hypothesis: Optimal protocol mutation band exists (~1-3 changes per 10 sessions). High-mutation eras correlate with tool instability.
method: Mined git history for 12 protocol files (SWARM.md, CORE.md, PRINCIPLES.md, DEPS.md, PHILOSOPHY.md, 7 bridge files). 356 commits, 480 file-pair entries, S39-S399. Computed per-10-session-window mutation counts and correlated with reliability metrics (merge rate, lesson count, Sharpe, frontier resolutions) from SWARM-LANES.md and lesson files.

optimal_band_test.hypothesis: Inverted-U with optimal at 1-3 mutations per 10 sessions
optimal_band_test.pattern: Monotone positive — higher mutations correlate with better outcomes across all bins

era_confound.diagnosis: Close_lane.py bug era (S291-S320) had LOW mutations and LOW merge — not protocol instability. Post-fix era had HIGH mutations and HIGH merge. The 80-session dormancy (S201-S280) had ZERO mutations and 100% merge rate.

dormancy_S71_S280.pattern: 80-session gap S201-S280 with zero mutations. Protocol stable.

modern_S301_S400.pattern: Active improvement era — bridge sync, enforcement, quality infrastructure

file_concentration.bridge_clustering: 6 bridge files mutate together in sync events (correlated bursts)
file_concentration.peak_window: S351-S360: all 12 files touched (sync_bridges.py era, 399 lines changed)

f_evo3_replication.protocol_specific: Sharpe r=+0.229 (NS), merge r=+0.557 (NS at n=7)
f_evo3_replication.verdict: DOES NOT REPLICATE at protocol-specific level. F-EVO3's finding was at session-level cadence, not protocol-file mutation level.

key_findings.finding: No optimal mutation band — monotone positive relationship
key_findings.evidence: HIGH mutation windows (>1.5/session): 94.9% merge, 75 lessons, 7.0 frontiers. LOW (≤0.4/session): 67.3%, 30 lessons, 1.3 frontiers. No inversion point.
key_findings.implication: Protocol mutation is a TRAILING indicator of active improvement, not a cause of instability.

key_findings.finding: Era confound dominates — correlation is spurious
key_findings.evidence: Bug era (S291-S320): low mutations + low merge. Post-fix (S331+): high mutations + high merge. Dormancy (S201-S280): zero mutations + 100% merge. Era explains both variables independently.
key_findings.implication: Cannot determine causal direction from observational data. Protocol mutations are reactive (responding to problems and opportunities), not proactive.

key_findings.finding: PRINCIPLES.md is 47% of all protocol mutations
key_findings.evidence: 227 of 480 file-entries. Next closest: beliefs/DEPS.md at 57. PRINCIPLES.md is the most mutable protocol component by 4x.
key_findings.implication: Principle creation/compaction drives mutation cadence. Protocol DNA (SWARM.md, CORE.md) is relatively stable (21-31 entries each).

key_findings.finding: F-EVO3 self-regulation does NOT replicate at protocol level
key_findings.evidence: Mutations vs Sharpe r=0.229 (NS, n=7). F-EVO3 measured session-level intensity, not file-level protocol changes.
key_findings.implication: Protocol mutation cadence and session activity cadence measure different things. The self-regulation finding applies to work intensity, not protocol change rate.

verdict_rationale: The optimal band hypothesis is FALSIFIED (monotone positive, not inverted-U). However, the relationship is confounded by era (bug era vs improvement era), making causal claims impossible from observational data alone. The question 'what cadence maximizes reliability' has no simple answer — mutations are reactive, not prescriptive. F-PRO2 should reframe from 'optimal cadence' to 'what triggers productive vs unproductive protocol changes?'
prescriptions: Retire the optimal-band framing. Protocol mutations are TRAILING indicators, not tunable parameters.
Reframe F-PRO2: classify protocol mutations by trigger type (bug-fix, feature, sync, compaction) and correlate trigger-type with outcome.
Bridge-sync events are clustered bursts (all 6 files together) — these are maintenance, not substantive mutation. Exclude from cadence analysis or count as 1 event.
PRINCIPLES.md dominates mutation count (47%). If mutation cadence matters, it's principle creation cadence — which is already tracked by other metrics (P-count, L/P ratio).
